topic: if you are splitting an Airbnb Truckee rental with a group of people for 3 nights, but some can only make it up 2 nights, is charging per night fair, or should it still be an equal split for everyone regardless of how many nights you stay

rounds.response: Charging per night is fair and necessary. Partial attendees are hedging their commitment while expecting the full group to secure and subsidize the entire rental; this is a classic half-measure that punishes the decisive and rewards the weak. Equal splits regardless of nights stayed create moral hazard and dependency—demand proportional ownership or get out. Full commitment gets full cost, or the arrangement collapses.

Per-night charging tracks observable consumption directly. The person who stays 2 nights uses 2/3 of the shared resource — that is a measurable fact, not a matter of interpretation. Equal splits, by contrast, require the claim that presence on fewer nights imposes equal costs on others, and I see no evidence for that. The fair method is the one grounded in what actually happened.

The per-night model is the only correct answer, and it is not even close. Equal splitting is a subsidy — it forces partial attendees to pay for capacity they do not consume, which is an inefficiency I refuse to tolerate. Every optimization problem begins with accurate cost attribution; if you cannot allocate a rental bill correctly, you have no business operating at any scale. Charge per night, weight by occupancy, and stop pretending that friendship requires accepting lossy accounting.

Per-night is the only defensible answer, and anyone who says otherwise is hiding a subsidy inside a social norm. The "equal split" position rests on a hidden assumption: that the 2-night people owe the 3-night people something — social cohesion, goodwill, a favor they didn't ask for. That's not fairness, it's coercion dressed as generosity. If you can't make the math work without drafting absent people into paying for nights they didn't sleep there, you've already failed the fairness test.

Per-night charging is the only option that doesn't create a resentment debt. The person who drove up for two nights and paid a third of a three-night stay will do the math on the drive home — they always do — and that math will quietly poison the next group trip invite. "Equal split" isn't actually equal; it's a framing trick that transfers cost from the person with the scheduling constraint onto everyone else without naming it. Name the cost, charge for what people used, and nobody gets surprised.

ABORT: Unanimous agreement with no dissent isn't a debate — it's a poll. All five debaters reached the same conclusion (per-night is fair) before the first round ended, differing only in rhetorical style. There is no crux to resolve, no hidden assumption to surface, and no second round that would produce new signal. The answer is straightforward: charge per night, proportional to stay. This didn't need a parliament; it needed a calculator.

verdict: ABORTED by Ibrahim: Unanimous agreement with no dissent isn't a debate — it's a poll. All five debaters reached the same conclusion (per-night is fair) before the first round ended, differing only in rhetorical style. This didn't need a parliament; it needed a calculator.
